content.text:
Most organizations trust their AI systems to answer questions about internal data.

But here's what they don't realize:

When a manager asks your AI copilot about compensation data, the vector database returns the most semantically relevant chunks.

Not the most appropriately authorized chunks.

Semantic similarity ≠ permission to access.

This is the AI security gap.
content.headline: The AI Security Gap

thread:
🧵 Thread: Why your RAG system is a security hole

1/ Your organization has IAM. You have SSO. You have ACLs.

But when you deployed RAG, you created a new access surface that bypasses ALL of it.

Here's what's happening 👇

2/ Vector databases retrieve based on semantic similarity.

They don't check who's asking.
They don't verify permissions.
They just return the closest matches.

3/ This means:

A junior employee can ask about executive compensation.
A contractor can query confidential roadmaps.
A departed employee's cached session can access current data.

The AI just... answers.

4/ "But we have metadata filters!"

Metadata filters require:
- Manual tagging of every document
- Re-embedding when permissions change
- Trust that users don't bypass them

They're a bandaid, not a solution.

5/ The real fix: permission-aware retrieval.

Evaluate access on EVERY query.
Check identity against policy.
Return only authorized chunks.

Same question, different authorized answers.

6/ This is what enterprise AI security looks like:

→ Identity-driven access
→ Runtime policy evaluation
→ Complete audit trail
→ No re-embedding ever

7/ We built this. It's called Gateco.

Free tier: 10K retrievals/month.
Works with any vector DB.

Try it: https://gateco.io

#RAGSecurity #EnterpriseAI

content.text:

content.text:
Your IAM policies don't apply to RAG queries.

Vector databases return semantically similar content—not authorized content.

This is the gap everyone's missing.

Two engineers ask your AI assistant the same question:
"What's the latest update on Project Phoenix?"

Engineer A is on the Phoenix team.
Engineer B is not.

Should they get the same answer?

In a traditional RAG system, they would.

In a permission-aware system, Engineer A gets the full context.
Engineer B gets: "I don't have information on that project for your access level."

Same query. Different authorized answers.

This is what identity-driven retrieval looks like.
content.headline: Identity-Driven Retrieval
content.cta: Learn how Gateco enables identity-driven access

thread:
🧵 Prompt-based security vs Identity-based security in AI systems

A critical distinction most teams miss:

1/ Prompt-based: "Don't answer questions about confidential data"

Problems:
- Jailbreakable
- No audit trail
- Can't enforce per-user permissions
- Relies on LLM compliance

2/ Identity-based: "User X can access documents with clearance Y"

Advantages:
- Policy enforced at retrieval layer
- Per-user authorization
- Every decision logged
- Can't be prompt-injected away

3/ The difference in practice:

Prompt-based: "I can't help with that" (maybe)
Identity-based: Confidential chunks never reach the LLM

One is a suggestion.
One is enforcement.

4/ Identity-based security means the LLM never sees unauthorized data.

You can't leak what you never retrieved.

#AISecurityInfrastructure #LLMSecurity

content.text:
"We can't change permissions—it would require re-embedding everything."

I've heard this from dozens of security teams.

And it reveals a fundamental architecture problem.

When permissions are baked into embeddings:
→ Changes require re-processing
→ Re-processing means downtime
→ Downtime means you don't make changes
→ Stale permissions mean security risk

The alternative: evaluate permissions at query time.

Change a user's access? Instant.
Revoke a document's permissions? Immediate.
Audit every decision? Automatic.

No re-embedding. No downtime. No excuses.
content.headline: The Re-embedding Problem

content.text:
"Changing permissions requires re-embedding."

This is the most common objection I hear.

But it's only true if permissions are baked into your embeddings.

Late-binding authorization = permissions evaluated at query time.

Change access instantly. No re-embedding ever.

When an auditor asks "who accessed what via your AI system?"

What's your answer?

❌ "We can't track that—it's just an API call to OpenAI"
❌ "The vector DB doesn't log queries"
❌ "We'd have to check application logs manually"

Or:

✅ "Every retrieval is logged with: user identity, query context, policy decision, returned documents, and timestamp"

Enterprise AI requires enterprise auditability.

Every. Single. Retrieval.
content.headline: The Audit Trail Question

thread:
🧵 "Who accessed what through your AI system last month?"

If you can't answer this in under 5 minutes, you have a compliance problem.

1/ Most RAG deployments have zero audit trail for retrieval.

Application logs might show queries.
But they don't show:
- Which documents were returned
- Why they were returned
- Whether access was appropriate

2/ What enterprise audit trail looks like:

Every retrieval logged with:
- User principal (who)
- Resource identifier (what)
- Query context (why)
- Policy decision (allow/deny)
- Timestamp (when)

3/ Why this matters:

- SOC 2 compliance
- HIPAA data access logs
- Legal discovery
- Security incident investigation
- Usage analytics

4/ The gap:

Vector DBs don't log per-document access.
LLM APIs don't know your permission model.
Application code would need to implement this manually.

Or: use a permission layer that logs by design.

#AICompliance #EnterpriseAI

content.text:
How Gateco works in 60 seconds:

1️⃣ QUERY
User asks AI system a question.
Gateco intercepts the retrieval request.

2️⃣ RETRIEVE
Vector DB returns top-K semantically similar chunks.
(Tenant-filtered, but not permission-checked.)

3️⃣ EVALUATE
For each chunk, Gateco evaluates:
- Who is asking? (Identity)
- What are they accessing? (Resource)
- What's the context? (Action + environment)
- What does policy say? (RBAC/ABAC rules)

4️⃣ FILTER
Only authorized chunks pass through.
Unauthorized chunks are blocked.

5️⃣ LOG
Every decision logged: principal, resource, action, verdict.

Result: Same query, different authorized answers.
content.headline: How Gateco Works

content.text:
Gateco in one sentence:

We evaluate permissions on every AI retrieval so your vector database doesn't have to.

Same question + different user = different authorized answer.

"Does this work with Pinecone?"
"What about pgvector?"
"We use Weaviate."
"We're evaluating Chroma."

Yes. Yes. Yes. And yes.

A permission layer shouldn't lock you into a vendor.

Your vector database is a storage decision.
Your embedding model is a retrieval decision.
Your security model is a policy decision.

They should all be independent.

content.headline: Questions to Ask

content.text:
Quick AI security checklist:

☐ IAM policies enforced on RAG queries
☐ Permission changes don't require re-embedding
☐ Every retrieval audited
☐ Different users get different authorized answers

How many boxes can you check?

Gateco checks all four: https://gateco.io

RBAC vs ABAC vs ReBAC for AI retrieval.

A quick primer:

🔹 RBAC (Role-Based)
"Engineers can access engineering docs"
Simple, predictable, easy to audit.

🔹 ABAC (Attribute-Based)
"Access if user.clearance >= document.classification"
Fine-grained, dynamic, context-aware.

🔹 ReBAC (Relationship-Based)
"Access if user is member of document.project"
Graph-based, reflects org structure.

Which should you use?

Usually: a combination.

Gateco supports all three—evaluated at query time, not baked into embeddings.
content.headline: RBAC vs ABAC vs ReBAC

content.text:
Every week there's a new AI data leak story.

Common thread: the AI had access it shouldn't have.

The solution isn't to stop using AI.

It's to ensure AI systems respect the same access boundaries as everything else.

Your IAM policies exist for a reason.
Your AI should follow them too.
content.headline: AI Data Leaks
content.cta: See how to prevent AI data leakage

content.text:
Hot take: Most AI data leaks aren't LLM failures.

They're retrieval failures.

The LLM did its job—it answered the question using the data it was given.

The problem: it was given data it shouldn't have had access to.

Feature spotlight: Policy Studio

Define who can access what in your AI system—visually.

→ RBAC: Role-based rules (Engineering can access codebase docs)
→ ABAC: Attribute-based rules (Access if clearance >= document classification)
→ ReBAC: Relationship-based rules (Access if user is project member)

No code required. Policies apply to all future queries instantly.

Try it in our free tier: https://gateco.io
content.headline: Policy Studio

content.text:
The difference between "AI can access our data" and "AI follows our access policies" is huge.

Most RAG deployments are the former.

They give AI broad access to organizational knowledge, then try to restrict what it says at the prompt level.

This is backwards.

Security should be enforced at retrieval, not generation.

Filter first. Then generate.

Not the other way around.
content.headline: Filter First, Then Generate
